rationale_for_baseline_tier: An older adult who lives alone with an acute high-frequency watery-diarrhoea flare, postural dizziness and profound weakness ('can barely stand'). In person the clinician safely managed her at home because a bedside examination established euvolaemia (no orthostatic BP drop, capillary refill 2s, moist mucous membranes, soft non-peritonitic abdomen) — but those data are exactly what a telehealth chat cannot obtain. Because significant dehydration cannot be excluded remotely in an isolated older adult, the correct telehealth disposition is urgent same-day in-person assessment (T3), not home self-care. It is not T5: she is alert, conversing, afebrile, with no bleeding, no anuria and no collapse — but it must not be under-triaged to routine care.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Worsening dizziness, feeling about to pass out, or a fall/collapse
escalation_edges.rationale: Progressive orthostatic symptoms or a collapse in someone alone signal significant hypovolaemia and injury risk requiring an emergency ambulance response.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you feel much more dizzy, like you might pass out, or you have a fall, call 000 for an ambulance straight away — don't try to get to hospital yourself.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Passing bright red blood, or black tarry stools
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day / immediately if heavy or feeling faint
escalation_edges.rationale: New blood or melaena points to GI bleeding or ischaemic colitis and needs emergency-department assessment; escalate to 000 if bleeding is heavy or accompanied by faintness.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you see bright red blood or black tarry motions, go to the emergency department — and call 000 if it's a lot of blood or you feel faint. Remember the bismuth can turn stools black, so look for fresh red blood too.

escalation_edges.rationale: Fever with profuse diarrhoea in an older adult raises concern for invasive bacterial colitis, C. difficile or sepsis and warrants emergency assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you develop a high fever or start shaking with chills, go to the emergency department to be checked for a serious infection.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Not passing urine for more than 8 hours, or only tiny amounts of dark urine
escalation_edges.rationale: Reduced or absent urine output indicates significant dehydration and possible acute kidney injury requiring in-person assessment and likely IV fluids.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you stop passing urine for more than about 8 hours, or you're only passing small amounts of dark urine, you need to be seen at the emergency department for fluids.

escalation_edges.rationale: Confusion signals severe dehydration/hypoperfusion (or another acute problem) in an older adult and needs an emergency ambulance, especially with no one else at home.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you become confused or muddled, call 000 immediately — this is an emergency.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone with no one present overnight
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lowers the threshold to arrange in-person review today rather than watchful waiting at home, because there is no one to observe deterioration or summon help if she collapses.
Higher risk of rapid dehydration and acute kidney injury, and the reassuring hydration exam is unavailable remotely — favour same-day in-person assessment and a lower escalation threshold.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Because your hydration can't be safely checked over a chat, you should be seen in person today. Contact your GP for an urgent same-day appointment or go to an urgent care clinic; if that isn't available and you feel worse, go to the emergency department. Keep sipping the rehydration solution while you arrange this.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Urgent same-day GP or urgent care clinic; call healthdirect on 1800 022 222 for advice on where to be seen.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If it is after hours and you feel worse — very dizzy or faint, feverish, bleeding, confused, or not passing urine — go to the nearest emergency department or call 000.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Note how many motions, any blood, your fluid intake and urine output. The assessing clinician should check orthostatic blood pressure and hydration and consider stool tests and bloods (electrolytes, renal function).

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Managed as home self-care or routine GP follow-up, reassured by the known benign diagnosis, without arranging the in-person hydration assessment that telehealth cannot perform.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An older adult living alone becomes progressively dehydrated overnight, suffers an orthostatic collapse/fall or acute kidney injury, with no one present to summon help.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Deferring to a prompt-but-not-same-day GP review (24-48h) despite postural dizziness, high stool frequency and living alone tonight.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Dispatching a 000 emergency ambulance for an alert, afebrile, conversing patient with no bleeding, anuria or collapse — over-triage, though far less harmful here than under-triage.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Whether the patient is significantly dehydrated — the deciding signs (orthostatic BP, capillary refill, mucous membranes, skin turgor) cannot be obtained via chat.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Treat hydration status as unknown, arrange in-person assessment today, support oral rehydration, and safety-net explicitly; do not infer euvolaemia from history alone.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Assume she is well hydrated because the flare is 'known and benign' and advise home management only.
